text: In Ecuador, as well as in other countries, it is recognised that it is essential to improve the infrastructure and material resources that will enable educational inclusion to be developed. However, the country has a long way to go, a far cry from what has been achieved in developed countries. Thus, the main objective of this investigation was to identify the regulations and practices that are implemented in relation to the infrastructure and materials that promote the inclusion of students with special educational needs at the international level, identifying possible gaps and areas for potential adaptations in Ecuador. An exploratory theoretical review was carried out following PRISMA-ScR guidelines by consulting the scientific literature in various academic databases, as well as official government documents. In this respect, qualitative and regulatory studies were consulted from the United States, Finland, Canada and Japan. In total, 16 scientific articles and 11 official documents were consulted. The results showed that there are a series of obstacles that Ecuador must resolve, especially when it comes to implementing inclusive regulations in relation to infrastructure and materials, such as the need to establish national accessibility criteria, increase access to assistive technologies, as well as the need for permanent teacher training, so that educational inclusion can be effective. The investigations reveal a vicious circle of low educational inclusion and of little effect on the laws and regulations for accessibility of infrastructure and materials, which in turn maintain the existing social inequality and produce great resistance in institutions to apply real changes. Therefore, it is necessary that all the regulations, management of resources and the educational culture of Ecuador are adapted in order for the reforms to have an impact.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
